title: Census glossary | Stats NZ
content:
16 September 2019, 12:00pm
Updated 23 October 2024
Descriptions of the terms we use when we talk about the census.
admin enumeration – use of administrative data to add people to the census usually resident population when a census response has not been received.
admin or administrative data – data collected by government or other organisations to operate, such as the collection of tax; to deliver health and education services; or to record events such as births, deaths, and marriages.
census night population count – count of all people in New Zealand on census night. This includes overseas visitors but excludes residents temporarily overseas. Usual residents temporarily elsewhere in New Zealand are included in the count for where they were on census night. For example, a person who usually lives in Christchurch city and is visiting Wellington city on census night will be included in the census night population count of Wellington city.
census response – is defined in two ways – as response to the census (unit response) and response to a question (item response). Unit response is when an individual returned an individual form or was listed on a household listing, but no individual form was received. To be counted as a response, valid values were required for two out of three of the fields: name, date of birth, and meshblock. A unit response is also when a household returns a dwelling form or a household set-up form. Item response is when a valid value has been given to a question that applies to the individual (on the individual form), dwelling, or household (on the dwelling form/household set-up form).
census usually resident population count – count of all people who usually live in and were present in New Zealand on census night. It excludes visitors from overseas and residents who were temporarily overseas. Usual residents who were temporarily elsewhere in New Zealand on census night are included in the count for where they usually live. For example, a person who usually lives in Christchurch city and was visiting Wellington city on census night would be included in the census usually resident population count of Christchurch city.
collection response rate – the field response rate at the end of the collection phase, divided by the number of people who should have been counted (DSE benchmark).
combined census model – combining data collected from census forms with alternative data sources such as admin data and historical census data to produce the census dataset.
confidentialise – the process of applying protection so that respondents’ personal or household information is not divulged nor can be deduced from published statistics or microdata. Census uses practices consistent with the United Nations Statistical Division definition of statistical confidentiality.
derived variable – a variable created by calculating or categorising other variables, rather than being collected directly from a respondent. For instance, the combination of responses to gender and sex at birth will determine the value of the derived variable ‘cisgender and transgender status’.
deterministic derivation – when a value from a census variable can be used to inform a missing value in another census variable. For example, a valid ‘Iwi affiliation’ value can be used to inform a value for ‘Māori descent’.
dual system estimate (DSE) benchmark – the estimated usually resident population count on census night. It is calculated using census responses and admin data to derive a new estimate of the population in New Zealand. Benchmark population estimates as at census night are created by age and sex, for key ethnic groups, and sub-national geographies, and are used to calculate coverage and response rates.
dwelling form – a form that individuals fill out in the census that provides information about their dwelling.
estimated resident population – an estimate of all the people who usually live in New Zealand at a given date. The estimated resident population of New Zealand includes all residents present in New Zealand and counted by the census (census usually resident population count), residents who are temporarily overseas (who are not included in the census), and an adjustment for residents missed or counted more than once by the census (net census undercount). Visitors from overseas are excluded.
extended family – a group of related people who usually live together either as a family nucleus with one or more other related people, or as two or more related family nuclei, with or without other related people.
family – two or more people (also called a family nucleus) living in the same household who are either a couple, with or without children, or one parent and their child or children.
full-field enumeration – a census model that uses a survey to enumerate or count the population and dwellings, without the use of an administrative register(s).
gross overcount – the number of people counted more than once in the census, and people who were counted who should not have been. This is measured by the Post-enumeration Survey.
gross undercount – the number of people who were supposed to be counted by the census but were not counted. This is measured by the Post-enumeration Survey.
guide notes – information included with paper and online forms that helps respondents to answer specific questions.
household – consists of one person usually living alone or two or more people usually residing together in a private dwelling. Households are classified according to the relationships between the people in the household. Household composition is based on how many and what type(s) of family nuclei are present in a household and whether there are related, or unrelated, people present.
household coding process – the process by which individuals are placed into households and families by the census processing system.
household listing – information from the census paper dwelling form or online household set-up form that lists the people who are normally resident in a dwelling or are present on census night.
individual form – a form that individuals fill out in the census that provides information about them.
interim coverage rate – the number of people who were counted in the census using census form responses and admin enumerations, divided by the number of people who should have been counted (DSE benchmark). The interim coverage rate is an early measure of coverage calculated before Post-enumeration Survey data is available.
item sources – how the attribute information for a variable is sourced. For example, from a 2023 Census response, historical census data, admin data, or statistical imputation.
missing and residual responses – represent data gaps where respondents either did not provide answers (missing responses) or provided answers that did not fit predefined categories (residual responses). Possible residual categories for responses that could not be classified or did not provide the type of information asked for are ‘Response unidentifiable’, ‘Response outside scope’, and ‘Not stated’. For some census questions, ‘Don’t know’, ‘Object to answering’, ‘Prefer not to say’, and ‘Refused to answer’ are valid responses, however, are generally considered residual responses (depending on the concept), as they don’t correspond to the concept being measured.
net undercount – difference between gross undercount and gross overcount.
metadata – information that supports the use of data. This includes such things as information by concept, methodological and conceptual papers, quality ratings, and data use guides.
parental data – for an individual, data from parents may be used to fill missing information for Māori descent, Māori descent electoral, and iwi affiliation. For example, a parent’s Māori descent can inform a child’s Māori descent.
partial response – information provided about an individual on the paper dwelling form or online household set-up form, for whom an individual form was not completed.
Post-enumeration Survey (PES) – a survey of a sample of households run after the census to measure the completeness of the census count. The accuracy of the count (undercount or overcount) as measured by the PES is used to produce the official census coverage and response rates.
repatriation – the process of putting a person back into their usual residence dwelling when they completed an individual form away from home on census night. Repatriation provides more complete household information for people who were away from home on census night, than the absentees record. If an absentee record exists for that person at their usual residence, their individual form and absentee record are linked to create one combined record. If no absentee record exists for that person, they are assigned to their usual residence dwelling.
statistical imputation – the process of replacing missing data with estimated values from a statistical process (in contrast to sourcing real values from admin or historical census data). The purpose is to provide realistic values for missing data for individuals. For the 2023 Census, the method used was nearest-neighbour donor imputation (NIM). This finds respondents with a response to the variable in question who are similar to the respondent with missing or unidentifiable data, and copies over their response for the missing variable. This method of imputation uses CANCEIS (Canadian census edit and imputation system), developed by Statistics Canada and used in their census as well as by many other statistical agencies around the world.
subject population – the people, families, households, or dwellings to whom the variable applies.
total responses – several census questions invite multiple responses, and each response is counted individually in tables presenting total responses. The sum of responses will be greater than the usual subject population for that variable, because people may be counted more than once.
unit sources – how we counted people in the census dataset: from responses on census individual forms, from partial census form responses, or from admin enumeration.
usual resident – a person who usually lives in an area (including New Zealand at a national level). This is a statistical rather than a legal definition generally based on a person’s self-identified usual address.
Related releases, available from 2023 Census collection and release
